examples.title: Requirements Validation Checklist Generator
examples.description: Algorithm to generate a validation checklist based on requirements characteristics.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: ListOfRequirements
 OUTPUT: ValidationChecklist
 
 FOR EACH req IN ListOfRequirements:
 checklist_item = {}
 checklist_item.requirement_id = req.id
 checklist_item.checks = []
 
 // Check completeness
 IF req.functional_requirements.empty THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("MISSING FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS")
 IF req.non_functional_requirements.empty THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("MISSING NFRs (performance, security)")
 
 // Check consistency
 FOR EACH other_req IN ListOfRequirements:
 IF req.conflicts_with(other_req) THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("CONFLICT WITH: " + other_req.id)
 
 // Check testability
 IF contains_vague_terms(req) THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("AMBIGUOUS: Contains vague terms")
 IF NOT has_measurable_criteria(req) THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("NOT TESTABLE: No measurable criteria")
 
 // Check feasibility
 IF requires_external_system AND NOT system_exists THEN
 checklist_item.checks.append("FEASIBILITY: External dependency missing")
 
 // Assign severity
 IF checklist_item.checks.length > 2 THEN
 checklist_item.severity = "HIGH"
 ELSE IF checklist_item.checks.length > 0 THEN
 checklist_item.severity = "MEDIUM"
 ELSE
 checklist_item.severity = "PASS"
 
 OUTPUT checklist_item
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how automated tools can assist requirements validation by systematically checking each requirement against validation criteria. The algorithm identifies potential defects in completeness, consistency, testability, and feasibility, assigning severity levels for prioritized review.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) - nested loop for conflict detection
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - storing checklist for n requirements

examples.title: Test Case Derivation from Requirements
examples.description: Algorithm to automatically generate test cases from requirement specifications.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: ValidatedRequirements
 OUTPUT: TestCaseSuite
 
 FUNCTION derive_test_cases(req):
 test_cases = []
 
 SWITCH req.type:
 CASE "RESPONSE_TIME":
 // Generate performance test
 test_case = {
 "type": "performance",
 "input": "simulate_load(" + req.target_load + ")",
 "expected": "response_time < " + req.threshold,
 "pass_condition": actual_time < threshold
 }
 test_cases.append(test_case)
 
 CASE "DATA_VALIDATION":
 // Generate boundary tests
 FOR boundary IN [req.min - 1, req.min, req.min + 1,
 req.max - 1, req.max, req.max + 1]:
 test_case = {
 "type": "boundary",
 "input": boundary,
 "expected": boundary >= req.min AND boundary <= req.max,
 "pass_condition": is_valid(boundary) == expected
 }
 test_cases.append(test_case)
 
 CASE "WORKFLOW":
 // Generate path coverage tests
 FOR EACH path IN req.possible_paths:
 test_case = {
 "type": "integration",
 "steps": path,
 "expected": "complete_without_error",
 "pass_condition": workflow.execute(path) == success
 }
 test_cases.append(test_case)
 
 RETURN test_cases
 
 // Main process
 FOR EACH req IN ValidatedRequirements:
 derived_tests = derive_test_cases(req)
 TestCaseSuite.add(derived_tests)
 
 RETURN TestCaseSuite
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates how requirements with clear, measurable criteria can be directly transformed into test cases. Requirements that cannot generate test cases indicate a testability defect that must be resolved. The switch statement shows different test strategies for different requirement types.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = requirements, m = paths/boundaries
examples.spaceComplexity: O(t) where t = total test cases generated

examples.title: Requirements Consistency Checker
examples.description: Algorithm to detect inconsistent requirements using formal logic.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 INPUT: RequirementsSet
 OUTPUT: ConsistencyReport
 
 FUNCTION check_consistency(req1, req2):
 // Convert requirements to logical predicates
 pred1 = parse_to_predicate(req1)
 pred2 = parse_to_predicate(req2)
 
 // Check for contradictions: (pred1 AND pred2) == FALSE
 contradiction = (pred1 AND pred2).is_always_false()
 
 // Check for redundancy: pred1 implies pred2 OR vice versa
 implication_1_to_2 = pred1.implies(pred2)
 implication_2_to_1 = pred2.implies(pred1)
 
 IF contradiction THEN
 RETURN "CONTRADICTION"
 ELSE IF implication_1_to_2 OR implication_2_to_1 THEN
 RETURN "REDUNDANT"
 ELSE
 RETURN "CONSISTENT"
 
 FUNCTION generate_consistency_matrix(requirements):
 matrix = 2DArray[size][size]
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO requirements.length:
 FOR j FROM i+1 TO requirements.length:
 matrix[i][j] = check_consistency(requirements[i], requirements[j])
 
 RETURN matrix
 
 // Execute consistency check
 consistency_matrix = generate_consistency_matrix(RequirementsSet)
 
 // Generate report
 contradictions = []
 FOR EACH cell IN consistency_matrix:
 IF cell.value == "CONTRADICTION" THEN
 contradictions.append(cell.req1_id + " <-> " + cell.req2_id)
 
 report = {
 "total_requirements": requirements.length,
 "pairs_checked": n*(n-1)/2,
 "contradictions_found": contradictions.length,
 "contradiction_list": contradictions,
 "status": contradictions.empty ? "CONSISTENT" : "INCONSISTENT"
 }
 
 RETURN report
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode represents formal methods for detecting requirement inconsistencies. By converting natural language requirements into logical predicates, the algorithm can mathematically prove whether two requirements can both be satisfied. Any detected contradiction must be resolved before the requirements can be considered validated. This approach requires requirements to be written in a formal specification language.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n² × p) where p = predicate complexity
